g.text: Error Recovery: Halt-on-First vs Skip-and-Continue\nInput: x @ y $ z — two invalid characters @ and $
Lexer state after error | CLEAN — no recursive frames
Re-sync mechanism | trivial — advance() moves next
Parser impact | zero — ERROR token is just a type
Memory overhead | zero — error IS the token
Theoretical basis | lexer is flat FSM; no stack
Lexer on bad char | consume 1, emit ERROR, next is clean
Parser on bad token | partial AST node? active grammar rule?
Parser recovery | panic mode, sync sets, productions
Lexer recovery | none — main loop IS the recovery
Stack depth on err | lexer: 1 frame | parser: N frames
Clang Strategy | Skip-and-continue: ships faster wins
Language server | collects all ERROR tokens from scan()
VS Code renderer | red squiggle at 1:3 AND 1:7
User experience | Truthful diagnostics vs misleading
Valid tokens lost | y, z, EOF — parser never receives them
Fix cycles needed | 2 minimum (one error per compile)
IDE squiggles | Only on @ ($ appears clean)
Developer time | HIGH — iterative single-error workflow
Errors reported | 2 of 2 (all invalid chars found)
Valid tokens lost | none — full stream delivered to parser
Fix cycles needed | 1 (all errors shown at once)
